course_title: Assessment for Learning and Teaching in the Primary Classroom

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate comprehensive and well-founded knowledge and understanding of current trends and expectations related to assessment, and the nature of the full range of assessment strategies and approaches and how assessment and reporting inform planning of effective learning and teaching.

Demonstrate application of knowledge and skills to practice through the design of criteria and standards rubrics linked to learning objectives, and use of moderation practices to support consistent and comparable judgments of student learning.

Demonstrate understanding of a range of strategies for providing timely and appropriate feedback to students, reporting to students and parents/carers, and purpose of maintaining effective records of student achievement.
Demonstrate reflection on nature and use of large-scale systemic assessment data and capacity to interpret such data to inform effective planning and teaching.
Demonstrate clear, fluent, and coherent communication skills consistent with personal, professional and academic conventions.

assessments.task_description:
Context

This task is informed by current trends and expectations of assessment in schools, and recognises teachers' assessment literacy, evaluation and design of assessment as integral to student learning and improvement.

It requires you to apply knowledge gained from course lectures, tutorials and readings on the nature and design of quality assessment, and its use for strategic purposes in teaching and learning (e.g., formal and informal, diagnostic, formative and summative approaches).

Task

Length: 1500 words (+/- 10%) and a reference list, with a completed generative AI/machine translation technology statement

Write an essay that analyses the nature and design of an assessment task, including marking criteria.

You will need to:

  • use one of the AT1 sample assessment options provided in Blackboard
  • support your analysis using a range of relevant literature on the nature of assessment and quality assessment design
  • write using academic prose, without bullet-points or images
  • use APA7 referencing conventions to acknowledge all sources
  • complete and include a generative AI/machine translation technology statement, using the template provided in Blackboard.

Please note, if you use generative AI and/or MT technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference use of generative AI or MT technology may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate use, referencing and acknowledgement are available via the UQ Library Guide Overview: AI tools for assignments and AI Student Hub: UQ rules for using AI in assessment.

Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (Graduate level)

5.1 Assess student learning

Demonstrate understanding of assessment strategies, including informal, diagnostic, formative and summative approaches to assess student learning.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Task 2, Part A: Hurdle task — moderation meeting
assessments.due_date:
7/10/2026

On campus event: all students required to attend for the duration of their timetabled lecture and tutorial.
assessments.task_description:
Context

In lectures, course readings and tutorial activities, you have considered why and how teachers regularly work as a community of practice, to quality assure assessment design and teacher judgements of student learning. You also may have observed or taken part in these professional conversations during professional experience placements.

This task requires you to engage in a practical moderation event with your peers.

Task

Length (duration): approximately 2 hours + facilitated preparation time, using students' timetabled lecture and tutorials

Facilitated by course staff, you will actively engage in a moderation meeting on campus, using unseen sample assessment, marking criteria and student responses.

This event will be:

  • informed by the attributes of quality assessment and processes for effective moderation
  • scaffolded to support your work and guide professional conversations.

You will:

  1. Work in groups to unpack and moderate an assessment task and its marking criteria.
  2. Individually, mark a student response to that assessment task.
  3. Participate in group moderation on the marked response, before presenting key findings.

Where time permits, more than one assessment response may be marked and unpacked for moderation purposes, supported by calibration opportunities led by course staff.

Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (Graduate level)

5.3 Make consistent and comparable judgements

Demonstrate understanding of assessment moderation and its application to support consistent and comparable judgements of student learning.

5.5. Report on student achievement

Demonstrate understanding of a range of strategies for reporting to students and parents/carers and the purpose of keeping accurate and reliable records of student achievement.
assessments.submission_guidelines: For this task, you are expected to:

assessments.assessment_title: Task 2, Part B: Hurdle task — individual written reflection
assessments.due_date:
14/10/2026 2:00 pm

Submit to Blackboard within one week of the Part A moderation meeting
assessments.task_description:
Context

For Part A of this task, you were required to actively engage in a practical moderation event with your peers, facilitated by course staff.

This is the second and final component of Task 2. It invites you to reflect on assessment moderation processes and their importance to evidence-based judgements on student learning.

Task

Length: one A4 page (minimum size 10 font) + reference list, with a completed generative AI/machine translation technology statement

Prompted by your engagement in the Part A moderation meeting, write your own one-page reflection on the role of assessment moderation processes.

Your response needs to:

>> reflect on your own learning and experience of these processes, focusing on their importance in

  • developing effective assessment tasks
  • teacher judgements and student learning
  • reporting strategies that may be used to inform parents and carers about students' progress

>> include a rationale on why it is important to maintain effective records of student achievement.

Using APA7 referencing conventions, you may refer to course materials and/or readings to develop your statements; however, this is not the focus of the response.

You must include a completed generative AI/machine translation technology statement, using the template provided in Blackboard.

Please note, if you use generative AI and/or MT technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference use of generative AI or MT technology may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate use, referencing and acknowledgement are available via the UQ Library Guide Overview: AI tools for assignments and AI Student Hub: UQ rules for using AI in assessment.

Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (Graduate level)

5.3 Make consistent and comparable judgements

Demonstrate understanding of assessment moderation and its application to support consistent and comparable judgements of student learning.

5.5. Report on student achievement

Demonstrate understanding of a range of strategies for reporting to students and parents/carers and the purpose of keeping accurate and reliable records of student achievement.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission without an approved extension request via myUQ will result in a failing grade.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Context

Integral to their professional practice, teachers are expected to analyse student assessment data (evidence of student learning); participate in professional conversations to moderate their judgements and feedback to students; and use this understanding to inform classroom teaching and learning. Teachers continue to develop these skills through ongoing engagement with their community of practice and formal and informal professional learning opportunities, including professional and academic reading on assessment practices and data.

As a culminating task for the course, this assessment recognises the importance of combining these skills to your development as a future graduate teacher.

Task

Length: 2500-3000 words + reference list, with a completed generative AI/machine translation technology statement

Write a practice-based research report that analyses a student work sample.

You will need to source your student work sample:

  • during your professional experience placement or from the range of Blackboard samples (available from Week 8)
  • using course guidance on the collection, use and acknowledgement of school documents and student work, as appropriate for this task

You will need to analyse your sample and develop your report using:

  • the sections detailed below
  • your own critical engagement with professional and academic literature
  • APA7 referencing conventions
  • the Blackboard template, to complete and include a generative AI/machine translation technology statement.

Please note, if you use generative AI and/or MT technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference use of generative AI or MT technology may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate use, referencing and acknowledgement are available via the UQ Library Guide Overview: AI tools for assignments and AI Student Hub: UQ rules for using AI in assessment.

1. Analysis of the sample as student assessment data

Analyse the nature of the sample as student assessment data (evidence of student learning) and summarise your findings:

— in relation to the

  • nature of the task(s) to which the student responded
  • purpose and use of other data forms, including large-scale, standardised data

— supported by professional and academic literature on different forms of student data.

2. Making judgements and providing feedback: valuing moderation processes

In making judgements and providing feedback on the student work sample, discuss why knowledge and experience of moderation processes are valuable to this process. Do this by:

— independently, assessing the student work and identifying and outlining the nature of the feedback you would give the student

— detailing how your process is informed by learning through and professional experience of assessment moderation. E.g., developing this knowledge and understanding:

  • in moderating samples of student work (Task 2) and, if relevant, in moderating assessment instrument design (Task 1)
  • supported by literature on moderating teacher judgements and providing feedback to students on their learning.

3. Developing a responsive teaching and learning sequence

Develop, outline and explain the teaching and learning sequence you would implement to respond to this student's learning needs:

  • which you have already identified through analysis of their work sample and determining necessary feedback
  • using lesson summaries rather than plans, e.g., key points for each lesson in the sequence: lesson focus; relevant content description/s; learning intention/s; key activity/ies and resource/s
  • supported by literature on how effective teaching and learning practices are informed by curriculum, student data (evidence of learning) and the purpose/s of providing feedback to students on their learning.

Australian Professional Standards for Teachers (Graduate level)

2.3 Curriculum, assessment and reporting

Use curriculum, assessment and reporting knowledge to design learning sequences and lesson plans.

5.2 Provide feedback to students on their learning

Demonstrate an understanding of the purpose of providing timely and appropriate feedback to students about their learning.

5.4 Interpret student data

Demonstrate the capacity to interpret student assessment data to evaluate student learning and modify teaching practice.

learning_activities.topic:
Welcome to the course — set yourself up to succeed

During Orientation Week, set aside some time to:

  • view the course Blackboard, assessment tasks and due dates
  • familiarise yourself with course contacts and expectations
  • complete course readings for Week 1.

General contact hours begin in Week 1.

Unless otherwise signposted, teaching and learning is designed for on campus engagement via the 2-hour lecture and your timetabled 2-hour tutorial.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 3(27 Jul - 16 Aug)
learning_activities.topic:
Understanding assessment and assessment design

As an important orientation to the course, Weeks 1 to 3 are key to your preparation and submission of the Task 1 Essay.

Drawing on current trends in assessment research, lectures and course readings will focus on foundational knowledge for understanding assessment and assessment design, including:

  • the 'what’ and ‘why' of different approaches to assessment, including assessment of, for and as learning
  • assessment and reporting in planning, teaching and learning cycles
  • setting the standard: the role of the curriculum
  • principles and attributes of quality assessment design
  • school-based assessment design and making judgements
  • assessment communities of practice.

Please note, due to the EKKA public holiday there will be no on campus lecture in Week 3 (Wed 12 Aug). Instead, Week 3 content will be made available online, via the course Blackboard.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Understanding assessment and assessment design As an important orientation to the course, Weeks 1 to 3 are key to your preparation and submission of the Task 1 Essay. Please note, due to the EKKA public holiday there will be no on campus lecture in Week 3 (Wed 12 Aug). Instead, Week 3 content will be made available online, via the course Blackboard.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 3(27 Jul - 16 Aug)
learning_activities.topic:
Analysing and critiquing assessment design

In preparation for your Task 1 Essay, our first three tutorials will focus on developing your knowledge and skills in identifying and evaluating quality assessment.

Drawing on foundational understanding from lectures and course readings, you will:

  • use and become familiar with the Australian Curriculum v9.0 and the QCAA's ACiQ v9.0 resources in the context of assessment design
  • use research-based quality assurance tools to analyse and critique sample assessments for the primary classroom
  • understand the importance of an iterative, backward design process in developing valid and accessible assessment, and its relationship to making reliable judgements of student learning.

Please note, due to the EKKA public holiday there will be no on campus tutorials in Week 3 (Wed 12 Aug). Instead, Week 3 content will be made available online, via the course Blackboard.

DUE DATE, 2pm Fri 14 Aug 2026: Task 1 Essay — analysing assessment tasks

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

learning_activities.topic:
Evidence-based processes and reliable data

As you return from placement, we'll consider more closely why making reliable judgements of student learning is an ongoing, evidence-based process. We'll also revisit expectations for the Task 2 Moderation Meeting and Individual Written Reflection, and turn our attention to the culminating task for the course: the Task 3 Practice-based Research Report.

Drawing on current trends in assessment research, the Week 9 lecture and course readings will return to:

  • the role of assessment in the teaching and learning cycle, including assessment of learning, as learning and for learning
  • understanding and implementing a range of assessment strategies, including informal and formal, diagnostic, formative and summative assessments

while focusing more explicitly on:

  • working with a range of formal and informal student data to inform planning, modify teaching and improve student outcomes
  • moderation processes and teacher calibration, and the importance of these to the consistency and comparability of teacher judgements
  • maintaining effective records of student achievement.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Evidence-based processes and reliable data As you return from placement, we'll consider more closely why making reliable judgements of student learning is an ongoing, evidence-based process. We'll also revisit expectations for the Task 2 Moderation Meeting and Individual Written Reflection, and turn our attention to the culminating task for the course: the Task 3 Practice-based Research Report. Drawing on current trends in assessment research, the Week 9 lecture and course readings will return to: the role of assessment in the teaching and learning cycle, including assessment of learning, as learning and for learning understanding and implementing a range of assessment strategies, including informal and formal, diagnostic, formative and summative assessments while focusing more explicitly on: working with a range of formal and informal student data to inform planning, modify teaching and improve student outcomes moderation processes and teacher calibration, and the importance of these to the consistency and comparability of teacher judgements maintaining effective records of student achievement.

learning_activities.topic:
Effective feedback and reporting

Drawing on the lecture, course readings and placement experiences, our Week 9 tutorial will engage you in strategies for effective feedback to students and reporting.

It will also provide key takeaways in preparation for the Task 2 Moderation Meeting and Individual Written Reflection, and the Task 3 Practice-based Research Report.

Focus areas:

  • moderation processes and teacher calibration, and the importance of these to the consistency and comparability of teacher judgements
  • the significance and nature of timely and appropriate feedback to students
  • strategies for reporting student learning to parents and carers.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

It will also provide key takeaways in preparation for the Task 2 Moderation Meeting and Individual Written Reflection, and the Task 3 Practice-based Research Report. Focus areas: moderation processes and teacher calibration, and the importance of these to the consistency and comparability of teacher judgements the significance and nature of timely and appropriate feedback to students strategies for reporting student learning to parents and carers.

learning_activities.topic:
Standardised testing and school-based approaches

In Week 11, we'll consider how schools use standardised assessments and data to measure and improve student learning, with key takeaways for Task 3.

Drawing on current trends in assessment research, the lecture and course readings will focus on:

  • the role of data from assessment of, as and for student learning in teaching and learning cycle
  • standardised testing, data and mapping of literacy and numeracy skills
  • National Assessment Plan - Literacy and Numeracy (NAPLAN)
  • the Australian Curriculum v9.0 Literacy and Numeracy Progressions
  • Year 1 Phonics Check and Year 1 Number check
  • standardised testing and mapping of content areas, e.g., NAP Opt-in assessments; PAT assessments
  • schools and other stakeholders and audiences for standardised assessments.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Standardised testing and school-based approaches In Week 11, we'll consider how schools use standardised assessments and data to measure and improve student learning, with key takeaways for Task 3. Drawing on current trends in assessment research, the lecture and course readings will focus on: the role of data from assessment of, as and for student learning in teaching and learning cycle standardised testing, data and mapping of literacy and numeracy skills National Assessment Plan - Literacy and Numeracy (NAPLAN) the Australian Curriculum v9.0 Literacy and Numeracy Progressions Year 1 Phonics Check and Year 1 Number check standardised testing and mapping of content areas, e.g., NAP Opt-in assessments; PAT assessments schools and other stakeholders and audiences for standardised assessments.

learning_activities.topic:
NAPLAN as formative assessment

Drawing on the Week 11 lecture and course readings, our tutorials will consider effective use of standardised assessments and data at the school-level, to measure and improve student learning, and modify teaching practice when needed, with key takeaways for Task 3.

Focus areas:

  • standardised testing, data and mapping of literacy and numeracy skills — National Assessment Plan Literacy and Numeracy (NAPLAN)
  • the importance of building students' assessment literacy and preparedness in meaningful and practical ways
  • school-based case studies of effective use.

DUE DATE, by 2pm Wed 14 Oct 2026: Task 2, Part B Hurdle Task — Individual Written Reflection

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L05

Focus areas: standardised testing, data and mapping of literacy and numeracy skills — National Assessment Plan Literacy and Numeracy (NAPLAN) the importance of building students' assessment literacy and preparedness in meaningful and practical ways school-based case studies of effective use. learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 12 To Week 13(19 Oct - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
21st century assessment and learner diversity

In Weeks 12 and 13, lectures and course readings will address 21st century shifts in technology, teaching, learning and assessment—and in this context—the significance of teacher knowledge, skills and communities of practice, in designing and implementing quality assessment for all learners.

These sessions will also include final takeaways for Task 3.

Drawing on current trends in assessment research, we'll focus on:

  • legislative requirements of assessment design and opportunities for all learners
  • approaches to assessment development, including differentiated instruction, UDL, CASE and culturally responsive co-design
  • access arrangements, reasonable adjustments and school assessment policies
  • the importance of strengths-based practices and building students' assessment literacy and preparedness in meaningful and practical ways
  • managing equity, access and authentication of student learning in assessment, including use of ICTs and generative AI.



Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

These sessions will also include final takeaways for Task 3. Drawing on current trends in assessment research, we'll focus on: legislative requirements of assessment design and opportunities for all learners approaches to assessment development, including differentiated instruction, UDL, CASE and culturally responsive co-design access arrangements, reasonable adjustments and school assessment policies the importance of strengths-based practices and building students' assessment literacy and preparedness in meaningful and practical ways managing equity, access and authentication of student learning in assessment, including use of ICTs and generative AI.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 12 To Week 13(19 Oct - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Healthy teaching, learning and assessment cultures

In our final tutorials, we'll capitalise on your learning across the semester, and use school-based case studies to develop your understanding of:

  • the role of assessment design and data in evaluating and providing for individual student needs
  • legislative requirements for all learners and equitable approaches to assessment design and adjustments for individuals and cohorts
  • the importance of strengths-based practices and building students' assessment literacy and preparedness in meaningful and practical ways
  • managing equity, access and authentication of student learning in assessment, including use of ICTs and generative AI.

These sessions will also include final takeaways for Task 3.

DUE DATE, by 2pm Mon 9 Nov 2026 — Exam Block Week 1: Task 3 Practice-based Research Report


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
